question: Israel x Lebanon diplomatic meeting by April 19, 2026?

fragility_points: A single postponement could be decisive because the deadline is close.
Reporting may overstate independence if several stories rely on one briefing stream.
Participant role or meeting format may end up ambiguous under contract wording.
key_assumptions: Reported planned talks convert into an actual qualifying meeting before the deadline.
Participants satisfy the official-capacity and authorization requirement.
Public reporting or government acknowledgment is explicit enough for clean resolution.
main_logical_chain: Cold base rate for Israel-Lebanon official diplomacy is low because such meetings are rare and politically constrained.
Recent reporting establishes a real near-term diplomacy pathway rather than pure rumor.
That raises the probability above the cold prior.
But the contract requires actual occurrence, in-person format, official authorization, and public confirmation.
Those execution and wording hurdles justify a meaningful discount versus the 71.5% market price.
payload.main_thesis: Planned or expected Israel-Lebanon talks make a qualifying meeting more likely than not, but the market overprices execution because the contract requires an actual in-person, officially authorized, publicly confirmable diplomatic meeting before April 19.
quote_anchors: This market will resolve to 'Yes' if there is a diplomatic meeting between representatives of Israel and Lebanon by the listed date, 11:59 PM ET.
official information from the governments of the Israel and Lebanon, and a consensus of credible reporting
payload.source_quality_view: Good recent reporting cluster plus authoritative market wording, but evidence independence is only medium and contract-application ambiguity remains medium-high.
strongest_disconfirmers: Expected talks are not the same as a completed qualifying meeting.
Ongoing strikes and war stress increase cancellation, delay, or ambiguity risk.
The contract excludes several near-miss outcomes such as remote or insufficiently official contact.
strongest_supports: Multiple recent reports indicate talks were approved, expected, or likely to be hosted soon.
The contract allows indirect in-person meetings, broadening qualifying pathways.
The event window is near term, so a live diplomatic process matters materially.
payload.timing_relevance: The contract resolves on April 19, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, so short-window slippage risk is unusually important.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether the expected talks have already occurred or are only scheduled.
Whether the final format will clearly be in person and diplomatically qualifying.
Whether public acknowledgment will be clear enough before the deadline.
payload.what_would_change_view: Official Israeli or Lebanese acknowledgment, or Reuters/AP confirmation that an in-person official meeting actually occurred, would move the estimate up; postponement, remote-only contact, or evidence that the contact was merely technical/security coordination would move it down.

fragility_points: Meeting shifts to remote or shuttle-only format.
Post-event reporting is too unclear to establish qualification cleanly.
key_assumptions: The April 14 Washington meeting occurs in person substantially as announced.
The named ambassadors or equivalent delegates are authorized official representatives.
Post-meeting public acknowledgment or consensus reporting is clear enough for settlement.
main_logical_chain: The contract requires one qualifying in-person diplomatic meeting by April 19, not successful peace talks.
Multiple credible sources report a deliberate April 14 Washington session with official Israeli and Lebanese representatives.
If that session occurs as described and is publicly acknowledged or consensus-reported, the contract likely resolves Yes.
Remaining downside is mainly execution risk before the meeting happens.
payload.main_thesis: A publicly acknowledged in-person Washington meeting between official Israeli and Lebanese representatives is concretely scheduled for April 14, making Yes more likely than the market implies if the session occurs as announced.
quote_anchors: Israeli and Lebanese envoys are expected to meet in Washington on Tuesday.
The first in-person meeting in the expected negotiations between Israel and Lebanon will take place on Tuesday in Washington.
payload.source_quality_view: Source quality is medium-high overall: Reuters and AP provide meaningful independent support, with Times of Israel and Al-Monitor adding contract-specific context, but the evidence is still pre-event scheduling rather than completed-event confirmation.
strongest_disconfirmers: The meeting was still scheduled rather than completed in the sourced reporting.
Active fighting and ceasefire disagreement create real postponement or cancellation risk.
Agenda ambiguity could create a smaller qualification risk if the event changes form.
strongest_supports: Reuters says Israeli and Lebanese envoys are expected to meet in Washington on Tuesday, April 14.
AP independently reports Netanyahu authorized direct negotiations and expected Washington talks the following week.
Times of Israel explicitly frames the planned event as the first in-person meeting and quotes public acknowledgment from both sides.
payload.timing_relevance: The decisive catalyst is the scheduled April 14 Washington meeting, five days before the April 19 deadline.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether final official acknowledgment after the meeting will be explicit enough for unambiguous settlement.
Whether the immediate meeting agenda is framed as ceasefire mechanics or formal peace talks.
payload.what_would_change_view: Official cancellation, a shift away from in-person format, or credible post-event reporting that no qualifying meeting actually occurred would push the estimate down materially.

fragility_points: Cancellation or postponement of the reported Washington talks.
A non-qualifying format such as remote contact or unclear shuttle diplomacy.
Insufficiently clear public acknowledgment after the event.
key_assumptions: Widely reported US-hosted talks actually occur before April 19.
The format is in-person and contract-qualifying, even if mediated indirectly.
Post-event acknowledgment or consensus reporting is clear enough for resolution.
main_logical_chain: The market is likely pricing real near-term official talks rather than vague diplomacy.
Those talks create a strong Yes pathway because mediated in-person meetings can count.
But the contract is narrower than 'talks likely' and still requires successful qualifying execution.
That leaves enough residual execution and wording risk to place fair odds below market.
payload.main_thesis: The market is directionally right that a qualifying Israel-Lebanon meeting is likely, but 71.5% looks somewhat rich because evidence supports imminent talks more clearly than confirmed contract qualification.
quote_anchors: Israel and Lebanon are expected to hold talks.
payload.source_quality_view: Contract wording is clear, Reuters is strong, but reporting independence is only medium-low to medium and completion was not yet directly verified.
strongest_disconfirmers: Planned talks are not the same as a completed qualifying diplomatic meeting.
Ongoing military escalation could delay, cancel, or blur the format.
Headline consensus may overstate true source independence.
strongest_supports: Reuters repeatedly reported expected or historic US-hosted Israel-Lebanon talks.
Multiple outlets converged on a near-term Washington meeting timeline.
Contract wording allows indirect in-person meetings via mediators.
payload.timing_relevance: The event window is short and the reported meeting is near-term, so most of the probability comes from imminent execution rather than broad diplomatic thaw.
unresolved_ambiguities: Exact format of the planned meeting.
How clearly post-event reporting will establish qualification.
Whether the apparent source diversity is genuinely independent.
payload.what_would_change_view: Official or Reuters/AP-style post-event confirmation of a completed in-person qualifying meeting would push the estimate up; cancellation, postponement, or remote-only contact would push it down sharply.

fragility_points: Qualification risk under strict contract wording.
Deadline sensitivity and execution risk within days.
key_assumptions: Current direct-negotiation signals convert into an in-person meeting before the deadline.
Reported participants would be authorized official representatives.
Public acknowledgment or consensus credible reporting will emerge in time for resolution.
main_logical_chain: Regional reporting suggests active official diplomacy and possibly an imminent meeting.
The contract is narrower than generic diplomacy and requires a qualifying in-person meeting plus public acknowledgment or consensus reporting.
Because current confirmation is still incomplete, probability should remain above 50% but below the market's confidence.
payload.main_thesis: A qualifying Israel-Lebanon diplomatic meeting by April 19 is more likely than not, but the market is too confident because qualification, timing, and confirmation risk remain meaningful.
payload.source_quality_view: Contract text is strong, but current event evidence is mainly regional media with medium-low independence and only partial direct official confirmation.
strongest_disconfirmers: Available evidence does not yet cleanly prove all contract conditions at once: in-person, diplomatic, authorized, and publicly acknowledged/consensus-reported.
Ongoing conflict creates delay, cancellation, or reframing risk.
Direct negotiations can exist without a contract-qualifying meeting.
strongest_supports: LBCI referenced the eve of a Lebanese-Israeli meeting.
Naharnet attributed to Lebanese FM Rajji a statement about direct negotiations with Israel.
Italy being ready to host negotiation meetings suggests real diplomatic infrastructure.
payload.timing_relevance: The market resolves on April 19, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, so even a real meeting after that deadline would still be No.
unresolved_ambiguities: Exact participants, venue, and in-person status of the anticipated meeting.
Whether governments will publicly acknowledge the event.
Whether reporting consensus would be strong enough if official statements stay limited.
payload.what_would_change_view: Official acknowledgment, multiple independent reports naming participants/venue, or visual/readout evidence would move me up; postponement, remote-only contact, or lack of confirmation into the deadline would move me down.

fragility_points: A post-meeting government statement could quickly invalidate the below-market stance.
Thin visibility into exact meeting structure is doing a lot of work in the estimate.
Official-source access was partially constrained by site blocking.
key_assumptions: Expected talks are not automatically equivalent to a completed qualifying meeting.
Current reporting leaves residual ambiguity on meeting format, participant authority, and acknowledgment.
No clearly documented earlier qualifying meeting has already settled the market.
main_logical_chain: The contract requires a deliberate, in-person, qualifying diplomatic meeting with public acknowledgment or consensus credible reporting.
Recent reporting credibly indicates Washington talks involving Israel and Lebanon representatives are imminent.
That makes Yes more likely than No.
But current evidence still leaves enough execution and contract-fit ambiguity that 71.5% looks too high.
Therefore a modestly below-market Yes estimate is the strongest credible variant view.
payload.main_thesis: The market is somewhat overconfident because imminent Washington talks are real, but current evidence is stronger on expected diplomacy than on a clearly completed contract-qualifying in-person meeting.
quote_anchors: first round of which is set to take place in Washington tomorrow
representatives of the two countries will meet in Washington, DC on Tuesday
payload.source_quality_view: Rule text is strong and clear; event evidence is meaningful and independently corroborated but still secondary and incomplete on exact contract-fit details.
strongest_disconfirmers: The market may be right that the remaining procedural boxes are likely to be satisfied once talks begin.
Indirect in-person mediated talks can still qualify, lowering the bar versus a direct summit.
No credible source was found explicitly denying that the meeting path is real.
strongest_supports: Times of Israel says first-round Washington talks are set for April 14.
Le Monde/AFP independently says representatives of the two countries will meet in Washington.
Contract allows indirect in-person meetings through mediators, which keeps Yes above 50%.
payload.timing_relevance: The key reported event is April 14 in Washington, comfortably before the April 19 11:59 PM ET deadline, so near-term execution details dominate the forecast.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether both sides' representatives will be physically present in a qualifying format.
Whether participants are clearly authorized to negotiate on behalf of their governments.
Whether either government will publicly acknowledge the event or media consensus will become clear.
payload.what_would_change_view: A government acknowledgment or multiple post-April-14 reports confirming an in-person meeting by authorized representatives would push the estimate up quickly; evidence of postponement or non-qualifying format would push it down.
